review_title: the The ending lands with a dull echo rather than a scream
review_text: As the seventh entry in my all time favorite horror franchise, Scream 7 arrives with enormous expectations, and I approached it as a critic rather than a lifelong Ghostface loyalist. The film still delivers an experience worth watching, but it is far from untouchable. There is admiration here, but also frustration simmering beneath the surface.Seeing Neve Campbell return carries genuine weight. Knowing the reasoning behind her comeback adds emotional texture, and her presence immediately restores a sense of legacy. With Kevin Williamson back in a prominent creative position, there is an unmistakable attempt to channel the sharp, self aware energy that defined the earlier chapters. That nostalgic current runs strongly throughout.The incorporation of select legacy killers is one of the film's boldest swings. On paper, it is a clever reflection of modern horror culture and fan obsession. In execution, however, announcing those returns early works against the suspense. Instead of being immersed in the unfolding mystery, I found myself waiting for certain appearances rather than engaging with the present moment. The surprise factor suffers when anticipation becomes distraction.The cast is impressively stacked, and the kill sequences are among the most brutal and meticulously staged in the franchise. The opening is explosive and instantly gripping, one of the strongest starts in recent memory. Yet the pacing begins to sag as the body count rises rapidly, creating confusion rather than sustained tension. Nostalgia repeatedly injects excitement, but it cannot fully compensate for stretches that feel slow and oddly unfocused.There is also noticeable disservice done to several returning players, particularly one longstanding character who receives shockingly limited screen time. For a franchise built on legacy, that imbalance feels disappointing. When the story should be tightening its grip, it instead drifts, flirting with the kind of stagnation that once divided audiences during Scream 4.The most significant stumble arrives with the Ghostface reveal. Without spoiling specifics, it is easily the most underwhelming unmasking the series has delivered. The motive feels thin, almost bafflingly so, lacking the psychological punch expected from this saga. It is a good film overall, with strong performances and intense sequences, but the ending lands with a dull echo rather than a scream. Fans may accept it, not out of satisfaction, but out of hope that an eventual eighth installment will sharpen the blade once more.

review_title: Some Cool Ideas in a Very Disappointing Entry
review_text: The Scream franchise has always held a special place for me. The original remains one of my all-time favourite horror films, and I've generally enjoyed most of the sequels-especially the fifth and sixth entries, which I consider the strongest follow-ups to the original. Like many fans, I was genuinely excited to see Neve Campbell return to the forefront as Sidney in Scream 7. However, despite my overall goodwill toward the series (and even my mild reservations about Scream 3 being the weakest until now), I have to admit that this is the first instalment I truly disliked. To me, it stands as the weakest film in the entire franchise.This time, the story centres on Sidney and her daughter, Tatum, who is now the same age Sidney was during the events of the first film-an idea that suggests a "history repeating itself" angle. Unfortunately, the execution leaves much to be desired. The motivations behind the events, as well as the way they unfold, often feel illogical and poorly justified. Without spoiling any specifics, the character decisions, twists, and reveals lack the cleverness and internal logic that the series is known for. In the days since watching it, I've struggled to piece together why many of the plot developments happen the way they do, which ultimately undermines everything.Given that Kevin Williamson, the writer of the first, second, and fourth films, both wrote and directed this entry, my expectations were quite high. Sadly, the final product feels rushed and uneven, as though it never fully came together creatively. While Williamson's legacy with the franchise is undeniable, his limited directing experience seems to show here, resulting in a film that feels surprisingly unfocused. The signature meta commentary that once defined Scream is also noticeably diminished, and the story lacks the sharp self-awareness that once set the series apart from other slashers.There are still flashes of what makes Scream enjoyable. A standout bar scene revisiting the "rules" of horror briefly captures the spirit of the franchise, but moments like this feel disconnected from the film's heavier, more melodramatic focus on Sidney's family dynamic. Tonally, the movie is inconsistent, with forced humour awkwardly inserted into an otherwise dour narrative. While there are some entertaining kills and a few fun moments with returning characters, the inclusion of AI as a central plot element ultimately creates more narrative holes than intrigue. In the end, Scream 7 isn't without redeeming qualities, but it is deeply disappointing. Fans of the series will likely still be curious enough to watch it, as I certainly would for a potential Scream 8, but for general audiences, this is one entry that can easily be skipped.
author: kjproulx

review_title: Even with a good pace, it's clear that the franchise has nothing more to offer beyond nostalgia.
review_text: Scream 7 works when it leans into its fast pace, more explicit violence, and Sidney's strong presence at the center of the narrative. The family dynamic brings an interesting freshness and helps create a sense of urgency, especially in her relationship with her daughter. The film is entertaining, delivers solid tension sequences, and maintains constant energy.The problem emerges in the final stretch. By relying too heavily on nostalgia and false leads, it builds expectations it cannot sustain. The reveal feels weak, with shallow motivations and little emotional impact. In the end, what seemed like a promising return turns into a chapter that entertains along the way but disappoints in its conclusion.
author: oNerdcall

review_title: As a Fan of the Franchise, I Was Left Very Mixed.
review_text: Scream 7 left me pretty mixed overall.As a longtime fan who grew up on this franchise, this was easily one of my most anticipated movies of the year. And while there are definite bright spots here, especially the kills with excellent sequences and a few bold swings, it ultimately falls flat in the big finale because of some really questionable narrative decisions.Starting with the positives: the first act and most of the second is pretty strong. The pacing is quick, the opening scene is honestly a great one, setting the tone for what feels like could've been a dangerous and nuanced iteration of Ghostface. The movie begins exploring themes of toxic nostalgia and AI, which is a smart direction for a seventh entry in a legacy horror franchise and currently very relevant. There's real potential there, but the problem is it never fully came full circle in a compelling manner. Those ideas just fell flat with the third act here, just not getting weaved together very well by the end.The real standout here is easily the brutality and kills. The gore is next level here and in my opinion the best in the franchise. There's a kill in the bar sequence that had my entire theater audibly reacting, and I genuinely think it's THE best kill the series has ever done. The tension building in some of the scenes and ideas explored here in these sequences were also a very great addition, adding to the memorability of this film.Seeing Neve Campbell back in the lead role was also a huge plus, and having Matthew Lillard reprise Stu was a surreal and fun moment for me, coming from someone who wanted him back since Scream (2022). It ties directly into the nostalgia theme -which again, could've landed so much harder if the finale was better.About halfway through, the movie pulls a pretty bold move, something I've always thought would be interesting to see in a Scream film. On paper, it's a fantastic idea and a real swing for the fences. The issue is that it ultimately feels like it exists mainly to justify the third-act twist and to subvert expectations for the sake of it. Great concept but a shaky execution that only exists to justify one of its third act ideas.And that leads to my biggest problem: the third act and reveal.The killer reveal, dynamic, and motive are, in my opinion, the weakest in the entire franchise and it's seriously not even close. The entire climax from the reveal to the final fight feels rushed and doesn't give the moment enough time to breathe, with everything happening at break neck speed. Instead of leaving the theater shocked or satisfied, I walked out feeling like it just didn't stick the landing.The character work doesn't help either. The arcs feel pretty one dimensional overall, with a lot of retreading rather than evolution. While Scream has never been strictly character study horror, this entry in particular feels narratively thin as it goes on. There are multiple big moments near the end that don't get any compelling resolve, and you can sense the rewrites in the structure when these moments do come.Overall, Scream 7 isn't a flat out bad movie to me, especially for the seventh entry in a slasher franchise. It has some of the best kills, bold ideas, and a bunch of fun and tension filled moments. I both understand the clear criticisms, and understand the love it's receiving, I'm just sort of in the middle on this one.I wanted to walk out loving it. Instead, I walked out thinking it was just okay, entertaining but flawed, and ultimately disappointing compared to its potential, hopefully my opinions will change on inevitable rewatch but for now, those are my feelings on Scream 7.
author: Grumpyzz

review_title: In-between.
review_text: SCREAM 7 was always going to have a tough job. It had to balance nostalgia with carving out its own identity, and that's a tricky line to walk, especially after all the behind the scenes drama post SCREAM 6. Losing Melissa Barrera and Jenna Ortega was a major shift, so bringing Neve Campbell back wasn't just fan service, it felt necessary. And she absolutely earned that payday.Initially, I enjoyed SCREAM 5 and 6 upon release. But with some distance and once the initial excitement faded, I realized they didn't stick with me the way I thought they did. For me, SCREAM is Neve Campbell. Maybe that's the millennial in me, but this franchise really does live and die with Sidney Prescott. That's not to discredit 5 and 6, there are some elements to like, but a lot of my attachment came from the thrill of revisiting this world after SCREAM 4.On a meta level, SCREAM 7 leans heavily into the nostalgia and retcon era, clearly nodding to the legacy-sequel wave that took off after 2018's HALLOWEEN. The film even comments on how hard it is for franchises to juggle old leads with new blood. You need fresh characters to evolve, but letting go of your defining final girl is easier said than done. Given how strongly I feel that Sidney is the heart of this series, that theme actually resonated with me.When it was announced that Matthew Lillard (Stu), Scott Foley (Roman), and David Arquette (Dewey) would return in some capacity, the movie started to feel like SCREAM ENDGAME. So...did it live up to that hype?In some ways, yes. In others, not quite.There are moments that genuinely work. The film plays with expectations and builds tension effectively. But the third act is where everything hinges, and that's where I'm split. It doesn't completely stick the landing, but it doesn't derail either. It lands somewhere in the middle.Courteney Cox has a smaller role this time, but she's reliably strong as always. Mason Gooding and Jasmin Savoy Brown help maintain continuity from the previous films. I was pleasantly surprised by Sidney's daughter, Tatum. She avoids the typical moody-teen stereotype, and Isabel May brings a grounded strength to the role. Her friend group, however, feels more functional than fleshed out, very much there to raise the body count, though the kills themselves are executed well.The marketing has been effective, and financially it looks like the film will likely perform well. But I'm unsure about its long-term word of mouth. It doesn't fully satisfy longtime fans, and I'm not convinced it's bold enough to completely win over newcomers either. It plays things a bit safe.And if we're being honest, it's not overly difficult to narrow down who Ghostface might be. The casting makes certain possibilities feel obvious. A few stronger red herrings could have elevated the mystery. Franchise creator Kevin Williamson returns in a writing capacity and steps into the director's chair as well. I was a little hesitant at first, considering he only has one prior directing credit, but outside of a few uneven moments, for the most part he handles it well. He was hired late on the spot for 7 and if there is an 8, and he's involved from the ground up, maybe that's where he could really put his full stamp on it. Also shoutout to Marco Beltrami's score.If the box office exceeds expectations and reception skews more positive than negative, a SCREAM 8 feels inevitable. That's just how Hollywood operates. But as a fan, I do think there needs to be an endpoint. Is there room for another story or two? Probably. But how many times can Sidney realistically endure this? At some point, the franchise risks becoming the very thing it once cleverly satirized.That "in-between" feeling is what defines SCREAM 7. It's somewhat bold, somewhat safe. Nostalgic but restrained. Entertaining, but not definitive. It flirts with meaningful fan service without fully committing, almost as if it's holding one final card for the future.What ultimately anchors everything is Neve Campbell. She brings weight, history, and emotional credibility. She makes it feel like SCREAM.Nothing tops SCREAM 1 and 2 in that order. After that, I'd still place SCREAM 4 third (those motives were ahead of their time and documentary level accurate, even if Emma Roberts and Rory Culkin overpowering everyone remains a stretch). Then I'd slot 7, followed by 5 and 6, and SCREAM 3 at the bottom.A good entry. Just not the ultimate one.7/10.
author: drawlife

review_title: How to destroy your franchise in one decision
review_text: Scream 5 did something amazing. It executed a requel flawlessly and introduced two new leads that we are fully invested in and want to follow. The characters of Sam and Tara, played by Melissa Barrera and Jenna Ortega, are a huge reason why this beloved franchise returned to prominence.For those who don't know, Melissa Barrera spoke out about something that we're not "allowed" to criticize. She showed courage we rarely see from celebrities. And Spyglass Studios fired her for it.Jenna Ortega had also publicly spoken out about the same issue in the past. So in solidarity with her on-screen sister, she quit the Scream franchise the next day. Then the directors walked as well. In summary, Spyglass Studios sided with something indefensible and destroyed everything they built with this new set of Scream movies.Even though I loathed their decision, I figured it was still possible to make something good and rescue the franchise. Difficult, but possible. Anything can be good if done right. So I went in with an open mind, hoping for another great entry in a movie series I love...But Scream 7 is terrible. I could feel it pretty early. It's like a pale shadow or imitation. I kept trying to be patient and give it a chance. But I just wasn't invested. At all.Part of the problem is that we need to be introduced to a new cast with a new main final girl. And that final girl is so lame. I don't think it's the actress's fault. The character is horribly written and difficult to root for. She can't hold a candle to Sydney or Sam/Tara.The movie is more predictable than any prior entry. It's also filled with stupidity, to the point of me rolling my eyes more than once.In general, everything that makes the Scream movies awesome is inferior in part 7. The firing of Barrera has to be one of the most bafflingly moronic studio decisions I've witnessed in my 40 years of movie fandom.(1 viewing, opening Thursday UltraScreen 2/26/2026)

review_title: Watchable, but that ending was bad.
review_text: Sidney Prescott is now living a quiet life in Indiana with her husband and daughter, Tatum. However, history inevitably repeats itself when a new Ghostface emerges and commences a fresh killing spree. With the past refused to stay buried, Gale Weathers is once again on hand to help Sidney navigate the familiar terror.Bringing back director Kevin Williamson was a smart move; the film possesses significantly more DNA from the original run than the last couple of entries. It successfully recaptures that particular 90s slasher vibe, aided by costumes and sets that feel authentic to the franchise's roots rather than a modern imitation. However, perhaps times have changed, or the format has simply been done to death since the first movie's epic arrival. It plays more like a straight-up slasher, missing much of the sharp wit and dark humour that once defined the series.The highlight is undoubtedly the opening. The sequences in the "murder house" took me right back to 1996, packing in a genuine sense of atmosphere that the rest of the film struggles to maintain. While the music is used to great effect-with certain tracks deliberately tugging at the heartstrings of long-term fans-the ending is likely to leave many viewers disappointed. We all knew AI would eventually feature in the plot, and while its inclusion doesn't disappoint, it does feel like a sign of the times.Neve Campbell continues to shine as the definitive "final girl." Time has been incredibly kind to her, and she remains the undisputed soul of the franchise. There is, quite literally, nothing new on display here, but for fans of the original, there is enough to satisfy. Between the heavy use of the nostalgia hand, the references to the past, and a familiar face or two, it's a perfectly watchable experience. It simply serves as another reminder of the current lack of imagination in Hollywood.6/10.
author: Sleepin_Dragon

review_title: "Scream 7" is the weakest film in the franchise.
review_text: "Scream 7" is a fairly average film. The beginning is somewhat weak, and the story is full of scenes paying homage to the first two films and references to parts 3, 4, 5, and 6, all aimed at appealing to nostalgia. This is how Kevin Williamson crafted a script that fluctuates between highs and lows. At times it's very slow, at others it's bland, but it also has a couple of good murder scenes, Gale Weathers' first appearance on screen in this seventh installment is memorable, and during Act 3 a final revelation that showcases the most insipid and boring killers in the entire saga. Tatum Evans's introduction isn't particularly interesting; the character is written in a clichéd and very boring way. If Williamson broke the rules of the slasher subgenre in 1996 and revitalized it, this time his style becomes completely clichéd. Kevin Williamson as director is very clueless, although a couple of scenes have good suspense, perhaps a stroke of luck or a fleeting inspiration from having worked with the master Wes Craven. Neve Campbell delivers a solid performance as Sidney Prescott-Evans, and while her return is celebrated, her affection for the character is lacking, for over a decade, she's made it clear that the "Scream" franchise isn't her top priority, and money is a convenient excuse for her return (which is perfectly within her rights) demonstrating resentment towards the saga since the public only remembers her for her character. Courteney Cox gives a ferocious and incredible performance as the ever-fierce Gale Weathers, demonstrating genuine affection for her character, and breaking the rules of the final girl stereotype, a true scream queen. Jasmin Savoy Brown returns as Mindy Meeks, delivering a somewhat irritating performance, while Mason Gooding, as her twin brother Chad Meeks, is decent but nothing special. Isabel May, on the other hand, comes across as very inexperienced as a survivor, not having what it takes to be a worthy final girl. The appearances of Mckenna Grace and Celeste O'Connor are wasted on characters that could have been better written. The rest of the cast delivers a range of performances: good, average, and bad. The cinematography is forgettable and at times resembles that of a low-quality television series. The deaths are gory, but only a couple are well done. The music also evokes nostalgia, featuring the most iconic themes from the saga. It's worth noting the absence of Melissa Barrera (unfairly fired) and Jenna Ortega as sisters Samantha and Tara Carpenter, respectively, and who have become iconic for their on-screen chemistry in parts 5 and 6. It is necessary to mention that Melissa Barrera as Sam achieved a memorable performance as a very tough final girl with killer genes who is hard to kill (something that Tatum Evans' character lacks). "Scream 7" is a simple and predictable film. This installment is totally inferior to the rest of the films in the saga.
author: jp_91

review_title: This is must definitely not my favorite Scary Movie.
review_text:
Scream 7 (2026) is the seventh entry in the Scream movie franchise that was written and directed by Kevin Williamson and it follows Sidney Prescott and her daughter, Tatum, being targeted by a new Ghostface killer. This movie has gone through a lot of controversy that left a bad taste in my mouth leading up to the release date, but I wanted to give the movie a chance because I love this franchise and every movie including the television series have been consistently great. Unfortunately, this movie was absolutely freaking atrocious and it was everything I didn't want to see in this franchise.Positives for Scream 7 (2026):
The best thing in this movie is the mother-daughter dynamic between Sidney and Tatum Prescott as well as the performances from Neve Campbell and Isabel May. There are some great kills that are very gory. That's all I've got to say for the positives.Negatives for Scream 7 (2026):
This movie is to the Scream franchise what The Rise of Skywalker (2019) was to Star Wars. This movie was clearly being used to nostalgia bait the fans to thinking it's a good movie when it really has no idea what it wants to be. The opening scene is awful and the people who are brutally murdered have nothing to do with the rest of the movie. You have characters like Gale, Mindy and Chad, who show up for a while and they disappear for a while with no explanation for their absence. The twins don't even act like the same characters from the previous movies. The movie references the events from Scream VI (2023) in a way that was annoying while making comments I found to be disrespectful and I felt personally attacked by them. There are horror sequences that look cool at first, but they make no sense with the events leading up to them. The movie tries to trick you into believing that Stu Macher is alive because everyone says, "We never saw Stu die" except we did see him die and those people are freaking idiots for even believing it. The killer reveal was awful and it was spoiled for me, but I didn't even care at that point and it was so obvious who it was. The motivation for the killer was atrocious which makes no sense at all and you can't tell me I'm wrong. And finally, I felt nothing while I was watching the movie until it ended and I was filled with so much anger and rage.Overall, Scream 7 (2026) is a massive dumpster fire that disrespected me as a fan of this franchise and it is the movie from 2026 so far that I hate the most with a passion.

review_title: Disappointing but at least Ghostface can pour a beer
review_text: Scream 7 was doomed from the beginning, regardless of its final product. Losing the natural story progression of Tara and Sam was a massive blow. The prior two films, Scream and Scream VI, laid some interesting foundations that this installment could have expanded on in meaningful ways.Instead, Scream 7 had to essentially reinvent the wheel, and that proved to be far too difficult. Rather than building on what was already working, the film feels like it is scrambling to reset itself.Without diving into specifics, it comes across as trying too hard with very little payoff. The story and characters feel lacklustre, and the core basics that made the previous installments so effective, tension, sharp meta commentary, and genuine emotional stakes, feel noticeably absent here.That said, Ghostface being Ghostface is always awesome to watch.
author: leestoych

review_title: We finally have a stinker
review_text: Seven movies into the franchise, and we finally get a Scream installment that's actually trash. Maybe this was the blood splatter on the wall all along - I mean, it is a horror franchise, after all, so a catastrophic entry was statistically inevitable. Still, that doesn't make it any less disappointing. For a series that has managed to stay surprisingly consistent over the years, this is the first time it genuinely feels like it has lost its identity.What Scream has always had in its favor is the luxury of not taking itself too seriously. The films thrived on sharp meta-humor; winking at the audience, poking fun at horror tropes, and occasionally even mocking their own legacy. They weren't comedies, but the genre-savvy satire was the trademark. Here, though, that clever self-awareness is traded in for lazy writing. The film constantly reminds us that we're in the metaphorical seventh installment and that this chapter is about "nostalgia bait", smugly acknowledging the trope before proceeding to indulge in it shamelessly. It's meta without wit, and commentary without insight.The characters are easily the worst in the franchise: hollow, one-note canon fodder designed purely to be killed. The action is mostly lackluster (save for one genuinely creative kill), and nearly every sequence hinges on characters making aggressively stupid decisions. But the biggest failure is the reveal. In a Scream movie, the unmasking of Ghostface is the payoff - and here it's random, pointless, and painfully obvious. The motivation is paper-thin, the red herrings are nonexistent, and midway through, a character appears whose existence only makes sense if they're one of the killers. It's a disastrous reveal that drags an already bad film even lower. Ultimately, this feels less like a misstep and more like the beginning of the end for the franchise.

review_title: The Queen is Back
review_text: Yes indeed everyone's favourite final girl is back after her Scream 6 hiatus, and it is so good to see her again. Don't listen to the negative reviews, Neve never disappoints, with this latest instalment another winner.Sidney is happily married in another small town far away from Woodsboro with her teen daughter mirroring her own teen years, complete with creepy looking boyfriend Billy Loomis-ing his way through her bedroom window.Another Ghostface is inexplicably gutting teens and burning OG houses down, with Sidney and her daughter in the crosshairs. Probably Campbell's best performance, with Courtney Cox again making us forget all about Monica Geller as Team Gail returns in awesome fashion. Loved it 7/10.
author: MFC9

review_title: Unfortunately the worst of the franchise
review_text: I was really looking for reasons to love this film as I'm such a fan of the Scream franchise. Unfortunately, it is far and away the weakest of all of them and an entirely forgettable entry.The story wasn't awful, and actually showed some promise. It was good to see Sydney back as she is such an iconic character, and her having a family was an interesting dynamic. Isabel May as her daughter Tatum was solid, but deserved a better written role. In fact, the whole mother daughter relationship could have been much better. There were some good Ghostface sequences and the slasher gore was top notch. I also really enjoyed the opening sequence, which was the high of the film and was top notch Scream work.However, so many of the story elements felt so lazy. The Ghostface reveal itself was boring and unsatisfying and didn't even really make sense. The roles of Gale and Chad and Mindy were pointless and added little to proceedings. The writing was so cliche and tired for the most part. For example, does anybody know a 17 year old who has their phone on loud? Come on writers of Hollywood, find better ways to move your story along than having a teenager have their phone ringing loudly. It's just lazy.A real shame, as having Sydney Prescott back at the helm should have been a slam dunk for this franchise, but they somehow managed to fumble it. There are a few highs in this film worth checking out, and fans of the franchise will need to watch it for continuity sake, but movie goer casuals might want to give it a miss.
author: ethanbresnett

review_title: Scream of Boredom.
review_text: As a slasher fan, I went to the theater with hope - and, unfortunately, walked out feeling like I had wasted my evening.Honestly, there was more suspense in a bowling alley.So what's the problem with the film?Its astonishing banality and emotional emptiness. The characters feel like cardboard cutouts, existing purely to fill space - there's no inner life, no depth. You don't believe in them, and, more importantly, you don't care about them. When someone gets killed, it barely registers - and that's a fatal flaw for a slasher.The dramaturgy falls apart: the midpoint drags so badly you start checking your watch. The staging feels like a first-year film school exercise - technically everything is there, but something essential is missing. The direction adds nothing new to the franchise's mythology, no fresh visual language or creative perspective. The plot is predictable, the conflicts merely exist without development, and the attempts to play on nostalgia feel more like exploitation of legacy than an evolution of it.This is yet another reminder that some stories deserve to end at the right time.If you truly want to experience the spirit of the franchise, it's better to revisit the earlier entries - from the original Scream through the fifth installment - or watch the Scream TV series with Willa Fitzgerald, where the tension and dramatic structure feel far more alive.Not recommended.
author: MovimanBond

review_title: Hardly groundbreaking, but still incredibly entertaining and fun to watch as always.
review_text: Scream 7 may not bring much that's truly new to the franchise, and that in itself feels like a wasted opportunity - especially with the return of Neve Campbell after she skipped the sixth installment due to a pay dispute. This should have been a groundbreaking entry, both narratively and visually.That said, it's still incredibly entertaining and fun to watch. I can't help but always have a great time with this franchise, and this installment is no exception.Figuring out who the killer is this time comes with a fun new twist that taps into a very current theme. It's genuinely intriguing, especially considering the high body count. However, while the mystery itself is strong, the revelation doesn't quite live up to it. The new killers aren't given enough depth to feel fully fleshed out, and their motivations don't feel as justified or impactful as they could have been.The film remains self-aware, as always, though a few creative decisions could have been handled better. Sidney's daughter, introduced as a new character, isn't particularly memorable, and I'm not entirely sold on the casting choice for the role.Ultimately, the main strength of the film lies in the nostalgia of having the original characters back together. It's nearly impossible to top both the legacy characters and the actresses portraying them - their presence alone elevates the experience.There's plenty of action and a generous number of kills to satisfy any slasher fan. And with the eighth installment already in the works, I'm excited to see where the franchise goes next.
author: fabiolpinheiro1993

review_title: Nonsense
review_text: Scream 7 Is a rushed complete waste of time. Its not suspenseful, scary, thrilling or contains any good set pieces the last two movies in the franchise had. There is no character development for Sydney, so Neve Campbell's deserved 7 million payday was for nothing. The only reason Scream 7 gets a few points if because 3 of the kills were solid. The rest were stupid characters you didjt care about being killed off in boring ways.The killer reveal is by far the worst in the whole franchise. The motivation to kill is absolutely ridiculous.I regret spending money on this movie.
author: bbevis-47954

review_title: Scream 7 Is an Insult to the Franchise
review_text: The attempt to keep this franchise going without the newer returning characters is painfully obvious throughout the film, they were the ones who gave the series new life, especially in the last two entries, for me, Scream VI still stands out surprisingly as the best in the franchise, it struck a strong balance between legacy characters and the newer generation, and that combination worked quite well in my opinion based on the last two previous movie.Now with Scream 7, the absence of Melissa Barrera and Jenna Ortega is impossible to ignore, without them, the film feels less like a natural continuation and more like a forced cash grab, worse than that, it feels insulting to the franchise, both the plot and concept is stupid and barely makes sense, the execution is horrible, the pacing is boring, the tension is nowhere to be found, this is supposed to be a Scream movie, and yet there's no real suspense.Neve Campbell is the only one who seems like she's actually trying, everyone else feels like they showed up, said their lines, and went home with a paycheck, and Stu? A fan-favorite psychopath, completely mishandled, instead of doing something bold or interesting, they did him dirty, there's no way most Stu and Scream fans are okay with this treatment, it's careless and honestly disrespectful.The killer reveal is horrible, the motive is even worse, the explanation makes it all collapse, by the end, i didn't even care anymore, the only reason i rank this slightly above Scream 4 is because it looks and directed way better than whatever Wes Craven did with the 4th one back in 2011, again, insulting.I'm not even a die-hard fan of this franchise, but this is one of the most obviously forced sequels i've seen in years, unless Melissa Barrera and Jenna Ortega come back, i'm not showing up for Scream 8.
author: HabibieHakim123

review_title: Scream 7
review_text: Scream 7 was an amazing experience. I had the enjoyable experience to be able to see this in a packed XD theater. It was packed with tons of scream super fans and gear. Clapping and cheering at different scenes is what makes seeing a movie with tons of people in a theater so special. And a big franchise like Scream is what makes it even more awesome. But now let's talk about the actual movie itself.Scream 7 is set in Pine Grove, Indiana. This is the new home of Sidney Prescott and her daughter Tatum. Ghostface has come to Pine Grove to end Sidney once and for all.The opening scene is one of the most important parts of a Scream movie. This one does a great job of setting the tone of this movie. It was really interesting to see how the Macher house had become this museum of all past killings in the franchise. This was certainly a call back to many of the movies that came before. The brutality and overall shock factor of this opening scene was something that I did not expect. Little did I know these were the tamest kills in the entire movie.For me, I felt like this movie had a different tone than its predecessors. This movie was more intense and suspenseful with less comedic relief. I'm not complaining about this at all, it's just cool to see them writing something different instead of the same formula over and over again. On that same tone, the use of ai in this movie was really cool to show how long these movie have been here for.The acting in this movie was on another level. Of course, the Scream Queen, Neve Campbell, was incredible. Courtney Cox brings the fire to the screen as Gale Weathers. The new casting of Isabel May was a great decision. She does a phenomenal job playing Sidney's daughter.Now for Ghostface and the kills. I won't spoil who ghostface is, but I will say I was completely wrong. The kills in this movie are really what separates this one from the rest. It really takes modern horror to another level. Ghostface is an absolute beast in this movie stopping at nothing for Sidney and his daughter. I felt like the ghostface mask had a nice update in this installment and it was very well done. Props to the costume crew.This all couldn't have been done though without the return of Kevin Williamson. He actually directed this movie along with writing it of course. He cooked up a great movie!This was such a fun movie with so many intense and awesome scenes that will be very memorable! Can't wait to rewatch it when it comes out on streaming!

review_title: It's that time again!
review_text: 10/10 might seem like an over the top score for a film that I found a few flaws in. Here's the thing though, if I have as much fun in the cinema as I did with this film, to give it anything less than a 10 would be wrong. This film was a blast, flaws be damned.The opening scene of any 'Scream' movie is very important. It sets the tone and gets you back into the swing of things. I liked the opening scene of 'Scream 7' a lot. The nostalgic throwbacks to events from the original, and the phone call with the "play along at home" trivia questions, all nice touches.From there we go into quite a lengthy period without much action while all the characters are set up. This probably couldn't been cut down a bit, it did drag a little.Once the killings begin though the pacing picks right back up and doesn't relent at all from there on out. There are some absolutely brutal kills in this movie. It wasn't afraid to get gory.The ending was a bit of a letdown I will say. Probably the worst finish a 'Scream' movie has had so far. It just didn't quite feel worthy of a franchise that has always finished its films in such epic ways.Still, as I mentioned I had an absolute blast with this movie and it's unlikely I'll have a better time in the cinema in 2026 than I did with this film. Bring on 'Scream 8'! 10/10.

author: masonwoods

review_title: Fun kills, but the dumbest Scream yet
review_text: 30 years ago, Scream revitalized slasher horror with a witty, self-aware film that delivered genuine thrills and a good mystery to boot. Scream 7 seems like the other side of the hill. While it has the trademarks and some great kills/gore, it's also a tired chapter with what is easily the worst reveal of the entire franchise. I'm not even sure what they were thinking with all this. If Scream '22 and VI showed the franchise still had some fresh blood left to spill, 7 shows that the franchise has run out of steam.It's great to see Sydney again, with Neve Campbell once again owing the film every time she's on screen, even she looks like she's done. Isabel May also does a good job as daughter Tatum, named after the character from the original movie. There's something endearing about the story revolving around one scream queen passing the baton on to the next generation. We got some of this from Scream '22, but here it's much more personal. Courtney Cox feels wasted here returning as Gale. Yeah, it's nice to see her, along with returning Chad and Mindy from the last two movies, but their presence feels forced for the sake of continuing tradition.The kills are definitely a highlight, continuing the bloodier and more brutal nature set by '22. There's at least two here that are highlights of the entire franchise and may compete for best of the year. If much of the film feels tired, these are at least entertaining enough to make the film enjoyable. Sadly, almost all of it, the mystery, the personal connections, the nostalgia baiting, all feel almost completely undone by the ever climactic reveal. I thought '22 had weak reveals, but this is easily the worst of the franchise and I honestly can't believe everyone thought it would work. It gives the feeling that the franchise is played out and I now wonder what we may have had if the film had continued what was set by the previous film. Sadly, we'll never know, but my hope is that it's done.
author: cadillac20

review_title: A sequel that should have been sharper
review_text: For me, Scream is what the Avengers films are for Marvel fans. I grew up with this franchise - the rules, Roger L. Jackson's phone calls, the iconic characters, the shocking reveals. So I went into Scream 7 excited, but also slightly afraid of being disappointed.And that's exactly what happened. The film wins me over during the first two acts, but completely loses me in the third.The opening scene is solid, even if it's not among the franchise's best. It has good pacing, clever dialogue, and effective jump scares. However, Ghostface burning down Stu Macher's house feels more theatrical than logical. From there, the film leans heavily on nostalgia, especially through Sidney receiving calls supposedly from Stu. The idea of AI manipulation versus a real return is interesting and timely, but it's a double-edged sword. It builds suspense while also stealing space from the classic Ghostface calls that define the saga. When this arc resolves, it does so quickly and without real impact.Isabel May is a strong addition as Sidney's daughter, and seeing Neve Campbell back with meaningful screen time feels right. The balance between the two generations works well, and their dynamic is one of the film's strengths. Unfortunately, the supporting cast lacks proper development. While more interesting than the group in Scream 5, the film doesn't build enough suspicion around them. That classic "anyone could be the killer" tension feels weaker here.On the positive side, Ghostface is particularly brutal. The bar kill sequence is one of the most memorable horror moments I've seen in years. The tension works, the suspense is well constructed, and even the jump scares are carefully executed.The real issue is the final act. The killer's identity and motivations feel forced and underdeveloped, and several narrative threads are wrapped up too quickly. For a franchise known for smart and surprising reveals, this ending feels like a step backward.Still, Scream 7 isn't a disaster. It entertains, delivers solid tension, and offers some strong moments. But it lacks the mystery and shocking reveal that define this saga. For fans like me, it's worth seeing in theaters - but it leaves the feeling that this seventh stab could have been much sharper.
author: pedroquintaoo

review_title: A Complex, Authentic Homage That Breathes New Life Into The Franchise
review_text:
Having just walked out of the theater, I can confidently say that Scream 7 is a remarkable achievement for a long-running horror franchise. While the film pays a beautiful, nostalgic homage to the original 1996 masterpiece, it manages to remain an entirely authentic and fresh experience. It references the classic tropes and plot structures of the first film, but it doesn't lean on them as a crutch; instead, it uses them as a foundation to build something much more intricate.This entry feels significantly more interesting and daring than its immediate predecessors. The narrative is layered with more characters and complex subplots, making the story feel denser and more rewarding. Unlike the last few installments, the "predictable" slasher moments are surprisingly scarce here-if you watch closely, the film rewards your attention with subtle details that hint at the larger mystery.The decision to have Kevin Williamson (the brilliant mind behind the screenplays of Scream 1 & 2) both write and direct was the best choice the studio could have made. There is no one more qualified to helm the 7th chapter of this legacy. Beyond the multi-layered storytelling, his directorial touch brings unique camera angles and seamless transitions to the horror sequences. The "clichés" are almost non-existent, replaced by high-quality suspense and genuine tension.Compared to the last two films, I
left the cinema feeling much more satisfied. It's a high-quality sequel that respects its roots while evolving the "Whodunit" formula for a modern audience. A must-watch for any Ghostface fan.

author: Masami-J

review_title: Scream is the ONLY horror franchise where all the films are great!
review_text: First of all, I have to say that I'm a massive and longtime fan of the Scream franchise and as you can all imagine, it was obvious that I was gonna watch this movie at the cinema. When I first watched the official trailer of the movie I liked it and I had high expectations about it. Eventually, I watched the movie and I can confirm that it was as GOOD as I had imagined. When I first read the movie synopsis, I thought it was very interesting and mysterious, but I wouldn't consider it an innovative movie because it's concept was similar to the previous movies. The storyline was very well-written and well-explained, even though it was simple and easy to understand. The characters were very interesting, well-developed and likeable. Regarding the "fresh blood" I have to say that all of them were great and I really liked the new friend group, even though I wish they had more screen time. The casting choices were great and their performances were incredible, I mean each actor/actresses did a great job of portraying their characters. The filming location of the movie was beautiful and gladly, the new town that this movie was filmed was perfect. I have to admit, it was a very scary movie and there were many jumpscares (even though, most of them were kinda predictable lol). Gladly, it was a very intense movie from start to finish, extremely suspenseful and there were many great mini attack/chase scenes during the movie. I mean, the opening scene, the auditorium scene, the bar sequence and Tatum's chase scene were the highlights of the movie. Regarding the killcount, I have to say that the death scenes were very bloody, brutal and gory AF. Also, this movie had many creative kills and there was one particular death scene that was insane AF, I'm not joking. In my opinion, the opening scene was very promising, scary, intense, brutal and I really enjoyed it, so much. The 2nd act was something like a love letter to the OG fans, since it was full of callbacks to the first movie, some great death scenes, nice mini attack scenes and funny moments. Regarding the 3rd act, I have to say that I have mixed feelings about it. I mean, the killer reveal was definitely unpredictable, but I don't think it was a memorable killer reveal and the motive was kinda mehh... Anyway, at least it was a great movie and I had so much fun of watching it! Last but not least, the cinematography and the score of the movie were both beautiful and very well done. Overall, "Scream 7" was a great addition to this iconic slasher franchise that was giving me major H20: 20 Years Later movie vibes, it had a great and memorable killcount, perfect chase scenes, funny moments, it was visually beautiful and I would definitely recommend it to everyone!

author: Allstar734

review_title: Lucky Number 7?
review_text:
LIKES:
The Jokes For The Most Part
The Character Development Of The Leads
Shot Beautifully
Decent Suspense At Times
The Acting Is Fine
Nostalgia Rings Out
Savage Kills Continue For Series
Pace Is Good
Summary:
Scream has always been ahead of the game since its creation, making fun of the horror genre, but still making a horror film out of it. Those jokes sink in quite well, coming back with a bite far more vicious than previous entities. The seventh installment is merciless in its insults. The self-indulgence of the jokes had me laughing up a storm, the extreme humor hitting at just the right moments with a well-timed quip, muttering, or some clever joke to drop the hammer of Scream's humor. You have to appreciate a darker comedic tone, filled with aggressive language and horrifying actions, to fully appreciate it, but if you can, you'll find yourself rewarded with quite a film.Past the comedy, the movie continues to appreciate its roots, diving back into the violent thriller we know this slasher to be. Scream 7 did not get blunted by all the political mess, instead embracing more of the new age technology to unleash Ghostface's wrath with as much savagery as they could muster. Moments of intense suspense are decorated with close calls as the new killer is trying to hack their way past any person to reach their goals. Some decent fights, standoffs, and games of hide-and-seek accomplish much, all with some of the most chilling (and cheesy) finishers that had the audience reeling. The new age technology brings great cinematography to keep things as hectic as possible, and the carnage is filled with that scream vibe you are looking for in this tried and true franchise. Nostalgia is very heavy in this film, and it seems to repair the damage mentioned earlier, and in a way, the movie worked by being able to tie the classic and modern together.Amidst all of this is decent character development, primarily through the two leads of Sidney and Tatum. This duo is going to get much of the screen time and serve as the focal points of advancing the story past the simple fight for survival. I found relevance in Sidney's story, and appreciated them trying to give her new levels past what has been a broken record on repeat from film to film. While it doesn't quite reinvent the wheel, the new tale is the next logical step in a story like this, and I liked the surprising depth Neve was able to bring. May's character is Tatum, and the daughter started annoying and cliché, but eventually managed to give me more than I expected. May brought the right amount of emotional backing, and I liked the way she evolved with the character, and I liked how they fashioned someone a bit more realistic, but not just stuck in one character arc. Together, they were able to launch a lot of the things I liked from this element, and feel this dynamic duo accomplished much with their acting. The rest of the group does a fine job as well, with Courteney Cox having some solid line delivery to bring that heat as only Gale Weathers can. And all of this works at a pace that was entertaining, but not too fast to cut the storytelling down too much to be frustrating.DISLIKES:
The Jokes Get Old After A While
The Character Balance Is Off
The Mystery Element Is Lacking
Predictable For The Most Part
The Cursing Gets A Bit Much
The Revelation Is A Stretch
Summary:
However, the movie is still all about the extremes, which sometimes got a bit out of hand for me. For one thing, the jokes get old after a while. The meta-humor becomes a running joke that I kind of got bored with, and after recently rewatching most of the Scream series, I was at my limit with the continuous badgering and jokes. Throw in the language getting a bit out of hand to the point that vocabulary becomes very limited, and you once again lose me quickly in that type of humor. These two elements can be a bit intense, and without being ready for that all over again, you might find this limitation enough to avoid this film.Past this, the story element still hasn't quite found the full splendor that we used to get with the first trilogy and the recent two films. Scream 7 is torn between the two worlds enough to make the story feel a bit blander than I had wanted, sacrificing the character development we were getting with Screams 5 and 6, and trying to fall too hard back into the old trilogy. To some degree, it does accomplish some good, but I think this tale was a bit calmer and cliché than most might have wanted. I found the mystery element lacking, and was able to predict most of the twists and identities of those involved with the movie. I found the big reveal a bit too much of a stretch, and finding it either a very grandiose poke at modern times, or trying too hard to be that Soap Opera-like hyper drama that seems to be very popular these days. Unfortunately, the character balance is still missing for me, as new and old characters feel somewhat wasted and too bland compared to the past people who tried to fend off Ghostface. Sure, the opening sequence victims, this is expected, but the rest of the crew felt lost and pushed to the back, with only these trivial moments giving them any merit. It felt like this was a very assertive means to throw you off, but I can say the other films were much more accomplished at juggling all the characters. Finally, this film may have some very extravagant kills that can be cheesy, but they are able to do some very disturbing elements to the film, so please use caution before venturing to see it.The VERDICT:
Scream 7 turned out better on many levels than I had anticipated from all the drama it faced during production. Despite not extending on the story I was starting to enjoy, it found a space between both worlds that should satisfy both spectrums of fans. Loaded with the visuals, storytelling, and character approach of the new and the nostalgia, campiness, and characters of the old, Scream 7 has almost the best of both worlds in this Thriller/Horror/Comedy amalgamation. I had a lot of fun with much of the movie, especially the character development being prominent, but not getting in the way of the primary gimmick of the franchise, and all in a pace that works to support that. But past the creative kills and self-deprecating humor is a film that may still be too lost in nostalgia to be an improvement. There are still some heavily contrived plot points, more so than the previous installments, and they get a bit silly in their attempt to follow the rules established by this franchise. The violence and cursing get a bit too much, and the jokes are sometimes a tad too forced into your face to remain funny the whole time. In addition, the revelation was a bit underwhelming for me, but that was because of the predictability of the movie and the obvious foreshadowing. Finally, the character use is one of the weaker elements of this film, as new and old characters don't quite have the full integration I wanted, several of them seeming to be set up for new stories to tell as the franchise tries once again to pass the torch. Factoring all this in, I think the movie is still worth a trip to the theater, and many very enthusiastic fans will enjoy getting to see this one in the splendor of the theater.My scores are:
Mystery/Horror/Slasher: 7.5
Movie Overall: 7.0.

review_title: Tries so hard to be good while being amazingly terrible.
review_text: Why do they try so hard but can't seem to alleviate the cheese? Courtney Cox is absolutely horrid. Shows up and brings up as many corny lines as she can. It's like the script hands her a checklist of callbacks and forced one-liners and says, "Go get 'em." Instead of feeling sharp and self-aware like the original did, the humor just lands flat and desperate.The franchise used to balance meta commentary with genuine tension, but here it feels like they're winking at the audience every five minutes just to remind us we're in on the joke. The scares are predictable, the reveals feel rushed, and the emotional beats don't have time to breathe. You can practically see the formula working overtime behind the scenes.
